content.problemStatement:
You are given positive integers NNN and DDD. You may perform operations of the following two types:


add DDD to NNN, i.e. change NNN to N+DN+DN+D
change NNN to digitsum(N)\mathop{\mathrm{digitsum}}(N)digitsum(N)
Here, digitsum(x)\mathop{\mathrm{digitsum}}(x)digitsum(x) is the sum of decimal digits of xxx. For example, digitsum(123)=1+2+3=6\mathop{\mathrm{digitsum}}(123)=1+2+3=6digitsum(123)=1+2+3=6, digitsum(100)=1+0+0=1\mathop{\mathrm{digitsum}}(100)=1+0+0=1digitsum(100)=1+0+0=1, digitsum(365)=3+6+5=14\mathop{\mathrm{digitsum}}(365)=3+6+5=14digitsum(365)=3+6+5=14.

You may perform any number of operations (including zero) in any order. Please find the minimum obtainable value of NNN and the minimum number of operations required to obtain this value.
content.inputFormat: The first line of the input contains a single integer T denoting the number of test cases. The description of T test cases follows. The first and only line of each test case contains two space-separated integers N and D.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, print a single line containing two space-separated integers — the minimum value of N and the minimum required number of operations.

sampleTestCases.explanation: Explanation: Example case 1: The value N=1 can be achieved by 8 successive "add" operations (changing N to 10) and one "digit-sum" operation. Example case 2: You can prove that you cannot obtain N=1 and N=2, and you can obtain N=3. The value N=3 can be achieved by one "add" and one "digitsum" operation, changing 9 to 12 and 12 to 3. Example case 3: N=1 can be achieved by operations "add", "add", "digitsum", "digitsum": 11→24→37→10→1.

solution.approach: To solve this problem, we can use a breadth-first search (BFS) algorithm. We start with the initial value of N and explore all possible operations (adding D to N and calculating the digitsum of N) in a level-order manner. We keep track of the minimum value of N and the minimum number of operations required to achieve it.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T * (log(N) + log(D))) — the time complexity is dominated by the BFS algorithm, which explores all possible operations in a level-order manner. The log(N) and log(D) terms come from the fact that we need to calculate the digitsum of N and D, which takes logarithmic time.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(T * (log(N) + log(D))) — the space complexity is also dominated by the BFS algorithm, which stores all possible operations in a queue. The log(N) and log(D) terms come from the fact that we need to store the digitsum of N and D, which takes logarithmic space.
